findings.summary: App.tsx nests 12 providers deep (QueryClient > Auth > UserSettings > Project > Realtime > Shots > GenerationTask > IncomingTasks > Panes > LastAffectedShot > CurrentShot > ToolPageHeader). Several of these have implicit ordering dependencies. This creates a rigid initialization chain where changing one provider affects all children.

ShotEditorProps has 42 props spanning identifiers, callbacks, dimension settings, navigation, cache functions, parent refs (including MutableRefObject callbacks), and UI state. Several props are commented as 'Currently unused'. Despite extracting settings to context, the component at 1,131 LOC remains the largest in the codebase.

98 of 384 TSX files (25%) exceed the 300 LOC threshold stated in CLAUDE.md. 15 files exceed 700 LOC. The top offenders include ShotEditor (1,131), VideoLightbox (799), VideoShotDisplay (797), TimelineContainer (793), InlineSegmentVideo (781), and CharacterAnimatePage (779). Many of these have mixed concerns (jsx_rendering + data_fetching + handlers).

20 components have prop counts exceeding 50, with ImageGrid at 74 props, MotionControl at 69, VideoPortionEditor at 67, BatchSettingsForm at 66, and MediaGalleryHeader at 64. This indicates deep prop drilling rather than context or composition patterns.

Three import cycles exist: a 60-file cycle through ActiveLoRAsDisplay > FileInput > HuggingFaceTokenSetup > LoraSelectorModal > ..., a 59-file cycle through DraggableImage > GenerationDetails > MediaGallery > ..., and a 2-file cycle between shots/index.ts and useShots.ts. The large cycles indicate shared/components has become a monolithic dependency graph.

Edge functions use three different Deno std library versions (0.168.0, 0.177.0, 0.224.0) and three different Supabase client import styles (esm.sh@2, esm.sh@2.39.7, npm:@supabase/supabase-js@2). This indicates functions were written/updated at different times without harmonization.

Browser-facing edge functions consistently have CORS headers and OPTIONS handling, but the pattern differs: some define corsHeaders as a const and spread it, others inline the headers. 9 functions (correctly) skip CORS as they are worker/service-role only. One outlier: calculate-task-cost uses inline service-role auth instead of the shared authenticateRequest function.

The shared auth module (authenticateRequest, verifyTaskOwnership, verifyShotOwnership, getTaskUserId) provides consistent auth patterns across 23 of 32 edge functions. The remaining 9 are correctly service-role-only with inline checks. However, all auth functions use 'any' for supabaseAdmin parameter (5 occurrences), and the JWT decode in authenticateRequest manually parses Base64 without signature verification (by design, as noted in comments).

171 occurrences of 'any' across 38 edge function files. Key patterns: all Deno declarations use 'declare const Deno: any', supabaseAdmin is untyped in all auth/helper functions, and complete_task's generation routing uses 'any' extensively for taskData and return types. The Deno declaration is unavoidable, but typed Supabase clients and proper task context types would eliminate ~60% of the any usage.

Error handling follows a clear, centralized pattern: typed error hierarchy (AppError > NetworkError/AuthError/ValidationError/ServerError/SilentError), centralized handleError() utility, and auto-categorization via categorizeError(). Frontend is consistent. However, edge functions use plain console.error/console.warn with ad-hoc error responses rather than a shared error handling pattern, creating a gap between frontend and backend error strategies.

Query keys are fully centralized in queryKeys.ts with clear naming, scope hierarchy, and both specific and broad invalidation targets. Settings resolution is unified through settingsResolution.ts. Task creation goes through a single createTask function with idempotency keys. These are strong architectural decisions that create coherent internal APIs.

250 test files for 980 production files (25.5% file coverage). However the distribution is severely skewed: shared/lib has 68/76 (89% file coverage), shared/hooks has 116/131 (89%), shared/contexts has 9/11 (82%). But shared/components has only 19/448 (4.2%), tools has 21/228 (9.2%), and pages has 0/28 (0%). No integration, e2e, or Playwright tests exist. The test campaign (commit 98f5d789) added 250 files and 2609 tests, but these are almost entirely unit tests on lib/hooks -- the entire UI layer is untested.

utils.ts is 149 LOC with 6 functions (cn, cropFilename, fileToDataURL, dataURLtoFile, getDisplayUrl, formatTime). This is acceptable but getDisplayUrl (47 LOC with localhost detection and cache-busting) is a significant URL utility that could live in its own module. No catch-all 'utils' dumping ground problem -- utilities are generally well-distributed across purpose-specific files (errorUtils.ts, taskCreation.ts, mediaTypeHelpers.ts, etc.).

Segment settings exist in THREE coexisting formats: new segmentOverrides (nested object), deprecated pair_* fields (flat), and legacy user_overrides (old nested format). The buildMetadataUpdate function writes new format while cleaning up old fields, and extractSettingsFromParams reads from all three with cascading fallbacks. 77 occurrences of '@deprecated' across 28 files. The migration is write-new-read-all but there is no evidence of a bulk migration to eliminate old format data from the database.

useStructureVideo.ts has 10 '@deprecated' annotations, maintaining both single-video and multi-video (structureVideos array) interfaces. The hook exports 'legacy individual accessors' alongside the new format. Similarly, ShotEditorProps documents 'Legacy individual accessors (for UI components that haven't migrated)' and useShotSettingsValue has 'Legacy interface' sections. Multiple deprecated re-export shims exist (edit-video/components/VideoPortionEditor.tsx, shared/hooks/useSegmentSettings.ts).

Strong signals of AI-assisted codebase maintenance: 573 ASCII section divider comments (// ====), 74 emoji comments in production code, 487 JSDoc @param/@returns annotations (many formulaic), 46 'This function/component/hook' preamble comments. Commit messages follow a mechanical 'Wave N' pattern with precise counts ('Wave 5d -- useImageGenForm decomposition + edge function hardening'). The test campaign commit ('250 files, 2609 tests') added high-volume but shallow unit tests. Documentation has consistent 'Source of Truth / Key Invariants' format across all docs. The codebase works well despite this, but the patterns increase maintenance burden through verbosity.

344 custom hook files exist. While hooks are the standard React composition mechanism, this number relative to 384 components suggests over-extraction in some areas. The ShotEditor alone imports from 16+ hooks. Some hooks like useTimelineCore (656 LOC), useTimelinePositions (703 LOC), and useTimelineOrchestrator (660 LOC) are themselves too large, suggesting the extraction stopped at the hook level without decomposing the hooks further.

Hook files use two naming conventions: kebab-case (use-mobile.tsx, use-debounce.ts, use-toast.ts, use-voice-recording.ts) and camelCase (useApiKeys.ts, useAutoSaveSettings.ts, etc.). The kebab-case hooks are a small minority (4 of 131 hook files) but they appear to be shadcn-derived. This is a minor but visible inconsistency.

supabase/types.ts at 2,573 LOC is the single largest file. It is auto-generated from the database schema and is expected to be large, but it is imported widely and any regeneration creates massive diffs. This is standard for Supabase projects but impacts code review quality.

Several deprecated re-export files exist that just forward to new locations (edit-video/components/VideoPortionEditor.tsx, shared/hooks/useSegmentSettings.ts, travel-between-images/hooks/useSegmentOutputsForShot.ts). These maintain backward compatibility but add confusion about canonical import paths. The storageKeys pattern shows similar drift (travel-between-images/storageKeys.ts says 'Source of truth is now @/shared/lib/storageKeys').
